summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically reviews Guitar Hero, declaring it potentially the best rhythm game ever made. He describes discovering the game through GameSpot's glowing review and immediately hunting down a copy. The post details the gameplay mechanics, the impressive guitar controller with its whammy bar and tilt detection, and the fantastic soundtrack featuring classic rock hits. Atwood shares the experience of setting up the game at work for an extended session that left him with blistered fingers. He also highlights the passion of the game's producers at Red Octane and Harmonix, who saw Guitar Hero as a way to get people excited about music through gaming.
key_points: Guitar Hero represents a significant evolution in rhythm games, combining smart difficulty scaling with an authentic guitar controller experience
The game's soundtrack features classic rock that spans generations, potentially introducing younger players to music from before they were born
The producers' vision was to make music accessible and fun through gaming, hoping to create the same cultural fever that games like Street Fighter II once generated
The game creates genuine physical engagement - Atwood literally got blisters from playing, echoing Ringo Starr's famous exclamation
Community engagement was central to the game's development, with producers actively participating in forums and events
money_quotes: Guitar Hero might just be the best rhythm game ever made.
But there's one big difference between other rhythm games and Guitar Hero: this one goes to eleven.
I got blisters on me fingers!
He said to me in the most emotionless tone 'man this is fun...'
I don't think clarinet hero would be the next big thing. If that's what it took, I'd be at the front of the line wanting to make the game.
Everyone fantasizes about being a full-on rock star. I think this game hopefully will get out there and touch people and get them to realize music is fun and accessible.
My hope for Guitar Hero is that it causes some a similar fever among youth where they'll just be talking about hammer on's, pull offs and classic rock from before they were born.
key_insight: Great games tap into universal fantasies and make complex skills feel accessible, creating shared cultural experiences that bridge generations.
